Return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) `cd .opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server && npx vitest run tests/handler-checkpoints.vitest.ts tests/tool-input-schema.vitest.ts tests/hooks-mutation-wiring.vitest.ts tests/memory-save-ux-regressions.vitest.ts` 2) inspect assertions covering missing, mismatched, and matching `confirmName` payloads 3) inspect assertions covering shared mutation-hook result fields in hook wiring and save responses | Checkpoint-delete tests reject missing or mismatched `confirmName`, tool-schema validation accepts matching payloads, and mutation-hook/save-path suites confirm the shared result contract remains synchronized | Test transcript + key assertion output for schema validation and shared result-contract coverage | PASS if the contract suites pass and the assertions confirm `confirmName` is required everywhere while mutation-hook result fields stay aligned across runtime and response boundaries | Inspect `handlers/checkpoints.ts`, `schemas/tool-input-schemas.ts`, `tool-schemas.ts`, `tools/types.ts`, and hook result type definitions if any contract diverges |
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:221654:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/17--governance/221-shared-memory-admin-identity-governance.md:29:| 221 | Shared-memory admin-identity governance | Confirm shared-space admin mutations bind to one configured admin identity, reject ambiguous hints or configuration, and return the resolved admin subject | `Validate shared-memory admin identity governance for shared-space mutations. Prove that matching configured-admin hints succeed, ambiguous actor hints are rejected, mismatched hints fail authorization, and missing or dual admin env configuration fails closed with the documented reason codes.` | 1) Start the MCP server with only `SPECKIT_SHARED_MEMORY_ADMIN_USER_ID=user-admin-1` configured, then call `shared_space_upsert` with `actorUserId: "user-admin-1"` and capture the resolved actor in the success payload. 2) Call `shared_space_membership_set` for the same space while sending both `actorUserId` and `actorAgentId`, and verify the request is rejected as ambiguous instead of being silently resolved. 3) Call `shared_space_upsert` or `shared_space_membership_set` with a mismatched caller hint such as `actorUserId: "user-other"` or an `actorAgentId` while the configured admin is a user, and verify `shared_memory_admin_identity_mismatch`. 4) Restart the server with both `SPECKIT_SHARED_MEMORY_ADMIN_USER_ID` and `SPECKIT_SHARED_MEMORY_ADMIN_AGENT_ID` set, rerun `shared_space_upsert`, and verify the request fails with `shared_memory_admin_ambiguous`. 5) Restart the server with neither admin env var set, rerun `shared_space_upsert`, and verify the request fails with `shared_memory_admin_unconfigured`. | matching `actorUserId` with a single configured admin succeeds and returns the resolved subject; dual actor hints are rejected as ambiguous; mismatched hints fail with `shared_memory_admin_identity_mismatch`; dual admin env vars fail with `shared_memory_admin_ambiguous`; no configured admin fails with `shared_memory_admin_unconfigured` | MCP tool transcripts for `shared_space_upsert` and `shared_space_membership_set`, server env configuration used for each run, and captured error payloads showing the reason codes | PASS if the mutation path only trusts one configured admin identity, rejects ambiguous caller input or server configuration, and returns the configured admin subject on success | Check `resolveAdminActor()` env resolution, confirm only one admin env var is set per success case, inspect schema validation for dual actor hints, and verify the handler is returning the documented authorization/internal error reasons |
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:221668:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/17--governance/123-shared-space-deny-by-default-rollout-phase-6.md:31:| 123 | Shared-space deny-by-default rollout (Phase 6) | Confirm shared-memory spaces require explicit membership and respect rollout/kill-switch controls | `Validate Phase 6 shared-memory rollout controls. Capture the evidence needed to prove Non-members are denied by default; explicit membership grants access; kill switch blocks access immediately even for existing members. Return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Create space with `shared_space_upsert({ spaceId, tenantId, name, rolloutEnabled:true, actorUserId:"owner-user-1" })` — exactly one actor identity (actorUserId or actorAgentId) is required; first create auto-grants owner access to the actor 2) Call `shared_memory_status({ userId:"non-member-user-2" })` for a non-member and verify no accessible spaces 3) Attempt shared-space save/search as non-member-user-2 and verify rejection/filtering 4) Grant membership with `shared_space_membership_set({ spaceId, tenantId, subjectType:"user", subjectId:"non-member-user-2", role:"editor", actorUserId:"owner-user-1" })` — actor must be an existing owner, exactly one actor identity required 5) Call `shared_memory_status({ userId:"non-member-user-2" })` and verify shared access succeeds 6) Flip kill switch with `shared_space_upsert({ spaceId, tenantId, name, killSwitch:true, actorUserId:"owner-user-1" })` and verify access is blocked again | Non-members are denied by default; explicit membership grants access; kill switch blocks access immediately even for existing members | Tool outputs for space creation (with actor identity), membership update (with actor identity), status checks, and shared save/search attempts across all three states; verify exactly one actor identity (actorUserId or actorAgentId) is passed on every upsert and membership call | PASS: Non-member denied, member allowed, kill switch blocks again, actor identity required on all mutations; FAIL: Shared access works without membership, kill switch does not stop access, or mutations succeed without actor identity | Verify membership rows in `shared_space_members` → Inspect rollout_enabled and kill_switch flags on `shared_spaces` → Confirm shared-space access path reuses governance checks → Verify actor identity is enforced on `shared_space_upsert` and `shared_space_membership_set` |
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:221687:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/11--scoring-and-calibration/198-temporal-structural-coherence-scoring.md:29:| 198 | Temporal-structural coherence scoring | Confirm structural penalties, bounded coherence deductions, and immediate retry behavior in the quality loop | `Verify temporal-structural coherence scoring in the quality loop using one well-formed control memory and flawed variants. Submit variants that are too short or missing headings, include a future-dated completion claim, and include self-referential or unresolved causal links. Confirm the coherence breakdown applies the expected structural checks and bounded penalties, and verify that low-coherence content is rejected or downgraded while the quality loop retry cycle stays immediate and bounded. Capture the score breakdown and return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Prepare a well-formed control memory plus flawed variants covering short or heading-less content, future-dated completion claims, and broken causal-link metadata 2) Run the quality loop or ingest path for each case 3) Inspect the coherence score breakdown and quality-loop decision for control vs flawed variants 4) Confirm penalties apply to temporal and causal-link issues without inventing broader chronology logic 5) For an auto-fixable case, capture the retry cycle and confirm retries occur immediately with no backoff and stop within the configured limit | Structural checks evaluate content presence, minimum length, and headings; future-dated completion claims reduce coherence; self-referential or unresolved causal links reduce coherence; low-coherence variants are rejected or downgraded; retry attempts are immediate and bounded | Quality-loop transcript, coherence score breakdown, control-vs-variant results, and retry timing evidence | PASS: coherence penalties appear for the flawed variants, low-quality cases are blocked or downgraded, and retry timing stays immediate and bounded; FAIL: penalties are missing, weak content passes without explanation, or retries show unexpected backoff or unbounded looping | Verify control content satisfies structural checks -> Inspect coherence penalty branches for future-dated claims and causal links -> Confirm rejection threshold or downgrade path -> Check retry loop configuration and attempt count -> Ensure broader chronology analysis was not assumed in the verdict |
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:221693:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/17--governance/122-governed-ingest-and-scope-isolation-phase-5.md:29:| 122 | Governed ingest and scope isolation (Phase 5) | Confirm governed saves require provenance and scope markers and scoped retrieval blocks cross-actor leakage | `Validate Phase 5 governed ingest and retrieval isolation. Capture the evidence needed to prove agentId,sessionId,provenanceSource,provenanceActor} metadata 3) Query with matching scope and verify hit appears 4) Query with mismatched user/agent or tenant and verify hit is filtered out 5) Review governance_audit rows. Return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Attempt `memory_save()` with `tenantId/sessionId` but missing provenance fields and verify rejection 2) Save with full `{tenantId,userId | agentId,sessionId,provenanceSource,provenanceActor}` metadata 3) Query with matching scope and verify hit appears 4) Query with mismatched user/agent or tenant and verify hit is filtered out 5) Review `governance_audit` rows | Missing provenance rejects governed ingest; successful governed ingest persists scope metadata; mismatched scope cannot retrieve the memory; allow/deny decisions are written to `governance_audit` | Save/search outputs + DB query of scoped columns + audit rows showing allow/deny decisions | PASS: Missing provenance rejected, valid governed save succeeds, cross-scope retrieval returns no hit, and audit rows exist; FAIL: Ungoverned save slips through or cross-scope retrieval leaks data |

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225863:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225869:Mex is a structured markdown scaffold with a TypeScript/Node.js CLI for persistent AI project memory. Instead of a database, it keeps project knowledge in navigable markdown files such as `AGENTS.md`, `ROUTER.md`, `context/*.md`, and `patterns/*.md`. Its defining mechanism is drift detection: eight static checkers validate scaffold claims against the real codebase without AI or token spend. Those checkers cover path validation, frontmatter edges, pattern index consistency, staleness based on git history, command existence, dependency claims, cross-file contradictions, and script coverage. Drift starts at score `100` and deducts `-10` per error, `-3` per warning, and `-1` per info. The scaffold is designed to grow after real work through pattern creation, targeted context updates, and current-state refreshes rather than one-time setup alone.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225882:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225890:5. Read `external/mex-main/src/cli.ts` next. Trace the user-facing layers in order: `mex init`, `mex check`, `mex sync`, `mex pattern add`, `mex watch`, and `mex commands`. Use this to anchor later claims about the relationship between setup, drift detection, targeted sync, and scaffold growth.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225895:10. Examine the next three drift checkers individually: `src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts`, `src/drift/checkers/command.ts`, and `src/drift/checkers/dependency.ts`. Capture the exact thresholds and matching rules, including how staleness uses both days and commit counts, how commands are validated, and how dependency claims are compared to manifests.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225904:    Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/mex-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around knowledge scaffold design, drift detection, staleness checking, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project memory.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225906:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225915:3. Are Mex's staleness thresholds (`30` warning days, `90` error days, `50` warning commits, `200` error commits) reasonable defaults, and how might similar thresholds be calibrated for `Code_Environment/Public`?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225920:8. How does Mex's targeted sync flow convert static drift findings into precise AI repair prompts, and could `Code_Environment/Public` adopt a similar "fix only what's broken" pattern?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225921:9. How do Mex's GROW rules and `pattern add` workflow support scaffold auto-growth after tasks rather than only during initialization?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225928:- Do analyze how scaffold growth happens after tasks through GROW rules, `pattern add`, targeted sync, and current-state updates.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225948:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:225971:- staleness thresholds and git-based freshness signals

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233910:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233916:Mex is a structured markdown scaffold with a TypeScript/Node.js CLI for persistent AI project memory. Instead of a database, it keeps project knowledge in navigable markdown files such as `AGENTS.md`, `ROUTER.md`, `context/*.md`, and `patterns/*.md`. Its defining mechanism is drift detection: eight static checkers validate scaffold claims against the real codebase without AI or token spend. Those checkers cover path validation, frontmatter edges, pattern index consistency, staleness based on git history, command existence, dependency claims, cross-file contradictions, and script coverage. Drift starts at score `100` and deducts `-10` per error, `-3` per warning, and `-1` per info. The scaffold is designed to grow after real work through pattern creation, targeted context updates, and current-state refreshes rather than one-time setup alone.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233929:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233937:5. Read `external/mex-main/src/cli.ts` next. Trace the user-facing layers in order: `mex init`, `mex check`, `mex sync`, `mex pattern add`, `mex watch`, and `mex commands`. Use this to anchor later claims about the relationship between setup, drift detection, targeted sync, and scaffold growth.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233942:10. Examine the next three drift checkers individually: `src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts`, `src/drift/checkers/command.ts`, and `src/drift/checkers/dependency.ts`. Capture the exact thresholds and matching rules, including how staleness uses both days and commit counts, how commands are validated, and how dependency claims are compared to manifests.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233951:    Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/mex-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around knowledge scaffold design, drift detection, staleness checking, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project memory.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233953:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233962:3. Are Mex's staleness thresholds (`30` warning days, `90` error days, `50` warning commits, `200` error commits) reasonable defaults, and how might similar thresholds be calibrated for `Code_Environment/Public`?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233967:8. How does Mex's targeted sync flow convert static drift findings into precise AI repair prompts, and could `Code_Environment/Public` adopt a similar "fix only what's broken" pattern?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233968:9. How do Mex's GROW rules and `pattern add` workflow support scaffold auto-growth after tasks rather than only during initialization?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233975:- Do analyze how scaffold growth happens after tasks through GROW rules, `pattern add`, targeted sync, and current-state updates.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:233995:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:234018:- staleness thresholds and git-based freshness signals
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:234019:- targeted sync prompt generation
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:234033:Rank findings in this order: drift-detection value for `Code_Environment/Public`, documentation-to-code synchronization impact, scaffold simplicity-to-maintainability ratio, staleness-signal usefulness, compatibility with existing Spec Kit workflows, implementation cost, and cross-phase differentiation from phases `001`, `003`, and `004`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:234040:- findings that classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:236398:545:**Startup/recovery surfaces:** `session_resume`, `session_bootstrap`, and the startup brief now report freshness-aware graph status instead of count-only health. Startup surfaces are intentionally non-mutating snapshots, so later structural reads may still differ if repo state changes.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:236405:601:Resume session with combined memory, code graph and CocoIndex status in a single call. Use when you want the detailed merged resume payload directly. The response carries freshness-aware code-graph status (`fresh`, `stale`, `empty`, `error`) instead of count-only health. For the canonical first-call recovery path on session start or after `/clear`, prefer `session_bootstrap`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:236409:612:Complete session bootstrap in one call. This is the canonical first-call recovery step on session start or after `/clear`. It wraps the full `session_resume` payload plus `session_health` and returns context, health, structural readiness and recommended next actions. Startup/bootstrap surfaces are freshness-aware but non-mutating; use `code_graph_scan` when readiness shows an empty or broad full-scan state.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:236419:779:Check session readiness: priming status, code graph freshness and time since last tool call. Returns `ok`, `warning` or `stale` with actionable hints. Call periodically during long sessions to detect context drift.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:237756:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/tests/trigger-phrase-sanitizer-manual-preservation.vitest.ts:15:      'DR-014-I001-P1-001 title description rejection',

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:238012:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/tests/memory-template-contract.vitest.ts:94:  it('rejects malformed trigger_phrases frontmatter shapes', () => {
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:238680:   543	**Read-path readiness:** `ensureCodeGraphReady()` runs automatically inside `code_graph_query` and `code_graph_context`. It checks graph freshness, returns a `readiness` block, and performs bounded inline selective reindex only when the stale set is small enough to repair safely on the read path. Empty graphs, large stale sets, and other full-scan cases remain explicit `code_graph_scan` work.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:238682:   545	**Startup/recovery surfaces:** `session_resume`, `session_bootstrap`, and the startup brief now report freshness-aware graph status instead of count-only health. .opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:238738:   601	Resume session with combined memory, code graph and CocoIndex status in a single call. For the canonical first-call recovery path on session start or after `/clear`, prefer `session_bootstrap`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:238749:   612	Complete session bootstrap in one call. This is the canonical first-call recovery step on session start or after `/clear`. It wraps the full `session_resume` payload plus `session_health` and returns context, health, structural readiness and recommended next actions. Startup/bootstrap surfaces are freshness-aware but non-mutating; use `code_graph_scan` when readiness shows an empty or broad full-scan state.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:238916:   779	Check session readiness: priming status, code graph freshness and time since last tool call. Returns `ok`, `warning` or `stale` with actionable hints. Call periodically during long sessions to detect context drift.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:239018:  1584	If it shows `INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT_ABORT`, add more real evidence. Returns `ok`, `warning` or `stale` with actionable hints. Call periodically during long sessions to detect context drift.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:239829:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:239835:Mex is a structured markdown scaffold with a TypeScript/Node.js CLI for persistent AI project memory. Instead of a database, it keeps project knowledge in navigable markdown files such as `AGENTS.md`, `ROUTER.md`, `context/*.md`, and `patterns/*.md`. Its defining mechanism is drift detection: eight static checkers validate scaffold claims against the real codebase without AI or token spend. Those checkers cover path validation, frontmatter edges, pattern index consistency, staleness based on git history, command existence, dependency claims, cross-file contradictions, and script coverage. Drift starts at score `100` and deducts `-10` per error, `-3` per warning, and `-1` per info. The scaffold is designed to grow after real work through pattern creation, targeted context updates, and current-state refreshes rather than one-time setup alone.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:239848:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:239856:5. Read `external/mex-main/src/cli.ts` next. Trace the user-facing layers in order: `mex init`, `mex check`, `mex sync`, `mex pattern add`, `mex watch`, and `mex commands`. Use this to anchor later claims about the relationship between setup, drift detection, targeted sync, and scaffold growth.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:239861:10. Examine the next three drift checkers individually: `src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts`, `src/drift/checkers/command.ts`, and `src/drift/checkers/dependency.ts`. Capture the exact thresholds and matching rules, including how staleness uses both days and commit counts, how commands are validated, and how dependency claims are compared to manifests.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:239872:15. .opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:241797:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md:601:Resume session with combined memory, code graph and CocoIndex status in a single call. .opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:241799:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md:612:Complete session bootstrap in one call. This is the canonical first-call recovery step on session start or after `/clear`. It wraps the full `session_resume` payload plus `session_health` and returns context, health, structural readiness and recommended next actions. Startup/bootstrap surfaces are freshness-aware but non-mutating; use `code_graph_scan` when readiness shows an empty or broad full-scan state.

When enabled, the save path performs 1 initial evaluation plus up to 2 immediate auto-fix retries by default. The reported `attempts` count reflects actual evaluations used, so early-break cases can stop before the configured ceiling. Accepted saves persist metadata fixes and carry rewritten body content in-memory until later hard-reject gates clear under lock. Rejected memories return structured rejection feedback instead of continuing to storage.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:253942:### Finding 5: Spec Kit measures memory quality and system health separately, but still lacks Mex-style freshness/integrity metrics
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:253943:- **Source**: [mcp_server/handlers/quality-loop.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/quality-loop.ts), [mcp_server/lib/validation/save-quality-gate.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/validation/save-quality-gate.ts), [scripts/lib/validate-memory-quality.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/lib/validate-memory-quality.ts), [mcp_server/handlers/session-health.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-health.ts), [mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-health.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-health.ts), [external/src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts)
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:253944:- **What it does**: Spec Kit’s memory-quality lane focuses on authoring/indexability: trigger quality, anchor quality, token budget, coherence, signal density, semantic dedup, contamination, and V-rule failures. Its system-health lane tracks session recency/recovery/graph freshness/continuity plus DB connectivity, vector availability, FTS consistency, orphan cleanup, alias conflicts, and embedding retry status. Mex adds a different missing lane: freshness/integrity truth, especially days-since-update and commits-since-update checks.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:253945:- **Why it matters**: The metric contract we should track is four-lane, not one-lane: `memory artifact quality`, `retrieval accuracy`, `live runtime health`, and `static freshness/integrity`. Mex is most useful as the missing fourth lane. Concretely, we should track quality-score distribution and gate failure rates; Recall/MRR/NDCG/ablation deltas; session/memory health status and repair backlog; plus stale-memory rate, broken-reference rate, and integrity drift rate.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:253946:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:253953:Define a unified Spec Kit metrics contract with four explicit dashboards: `artifact_quality`, `retrieval_eval`, `live_retrieval_observability`, and `freshness_integrity`. The best next iteration is to specify exact metric names, storage locations, thresholds, and attribution rules so Mex-style drift scores become one integrity lane inside a broader benchmark system rather than the benchmark itself.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:253969:- **Why it matters**: This is a strong integrity KPI for markdown truthfulness and staleness repair, but it only measures scaffold health. It does not measure retrieval relevance, answer quality, or runtime readiness.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254065:- **What it does**: Mex keeps the public surface to a few verbs: `setup`, `check`, `sync`, `init`, `pattern add`, `watch`, `commands`. It also fails crisply when you are inside `.mex`, when the scaffold is incomplete, or when there is no git repo. The `commands` subcommand doubles as a built-in cheat sheet.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254067:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254074:Define a concrete `spec-kit doctor` DX surface that maps existing capabilities into one guided flow: `bootstrap -> diagnose -> repair suggestion -> optional auto-repair -> recheck -> next call hints`. The highest-value follow-up is to design that command contract around current tools we already have: `session_bootstrap`, `memory_health`, `memory_save(dryRun)`, `code_graph_scan`, `memory_index_scan`, and trace-enabled retrieval responses.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254082:- **Recommendation**: adopt now / prototype later / reject / NEW FEATURE

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254116:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254122:Mex is a structured markdown scaffold with a TypeScript/Node.js CLI for persistent AI project memory. Instead of a database, it keeps project knowledge in navigable markdown files such as `AGENTS.md`, `ROUTER.md`, `context/*.md`, and `patterns/*.md`. Its defining mechanism is drift detection: eight static checkers validate scaffold claims against the real codebase without AI or token spend. Those checkers cover path validation, frontmatter edges, pattern index consistency, staleness based on git history, command existence, dependency claims, cross-file contradictions, and script coverage. Drift starts at score `100` and deducts `-10` per error, `-3` per warning, and `-1` per info. The scaffold is designed to grow after real work through pattern creation, targeted context updates, and current-state refreshes rather than one-time setup alone.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254135:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254143:5. Read `external/mex-main/src/cli.ts` next. Trace the user-facing layers in order: `mex init`, `mex check`, `mex sync`, `mex pattern add`, `mex watch`, and `mex commands`. Use this to anchor later claims about the relationship between setup, drift detection, targeted sync, and scaffold growth.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:254148:10. .opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:257398:The checker layer is clear now: Mex really is two different systems glued together, a zero-token static integrity pass and a separate AI repair loop. I’m pulling the matching Spec Kit Memory evidence next so the accept/reject calls are anchored in what we already have instead of hand-wavy overlap claims.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:257438:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md:543:**Read-path readiness:** `ensureCodeGraphReady()` runs automatically inside `code_graph_query` and `code_graph_context`. It checks graph freshness, returns a `readiness` block, and performs bounded inline selective reindex only when the stale set is small enough to repair safely on the read path. Empty graphs, large stale sets, and other full-scan cases remain explicit `code_graph_scan` work.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:257439:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md:545:**Startup/recovery surfaces:** `session_resume`, `session_bootstrap`, and the startup brief now report freshness-aware graph status instead of count-only health. Startup surfaces are intentionally non-mutating snapshots, so later structural reads may still differ if repo state changes.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:257444:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md:601:Resume session with combined memory, code graph and CocoIndex status in a single call. Use when you want the detailed merged resume payload directly. The response carries freshness-aware code-graph status (`fresh`, `stale`, `empty`, `error`) instead of count-only health. For the canonical first-call recovery path on session start or after `/clear`, prefer `session_bootstrap`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:258257:   543	**Read-path readiness:** `ensureCodeGraphReady()` runs automatically inside `code_graph_query` and `code_graph_context`. It checks graph freshness, returns a `readiness` block, and performs bounded inline selective reindex only when the stale set is small enough to repair safely on the read path. Empty graphs, large stale sets, and other full-scan cases remain explicit `code_graph_scan` work.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:258259:   545	**Startup/recovery surfaces:** `session_resume`, `session_bootstrap`, and the startup brief now report freshness-aware graph status instead of count-only health. .opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:258315:   601	Resume session with combined memory, code graph and CocoIndex status in a single call. Use when you want the detailed merged resume payload directly. The response carries freshness-aware code-graph status (`fresh`, `stale`, `empty`, `error`) instead of count-only health. For the canonical first-call recovery path on session start or after `/clear`, prefer `session_bootstrap`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:258326:   612	Complete session bootstrap in one call. This is the canonical first-call recovery step on session start or after `/clear`. It wraps the full `session_resume` payload plus `session_health` and returns context, health, structural readiness and recommended next actions. Startup/bootstrap surfaces are freshness-aware but non-mutating; use `code_graph_scan` when readiness shows an empty or broad full-scan state.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260405:Design the Spec Kit policy split explicitly: keep Mex-style static drift as a separate integrity layer, not part of recall, then define the retrieval policy matrix with four outcomes: `lexical-first`, `semantic-first`, `hybrid-balanced`, and `graph-first`, plus when a future `spec-kit doctor` facade should invoke each path.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260416:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260422:Mex is a structured markdown scaffold with a TypeScript/Node.js CLI for persistent AI project memory. Instead of a database, it keeps project knowledge in navigable markdown files such as `AGENTS.md`, `ROUTER.md`, `context/*.md`, and `patterns/*.md`. Its defining mechanism is drift detection: eight static checkers validate scaffold claims against the real codebase without AI or token spend. Those checkers cover path validation, frontmatter edges, pattern index consistency, staleness based on git history, command existence, dependency claims, cross-file contradictions, and script coverage. Drift starts at score `100` and deducts `-10` per error, `-3` per warning, and `-1` per info. The scaffold is designed to grow after real work through pattern creation, targeted context updates, and current-state refreshes rather than one-time setup alone.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260435:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260443:5. Read `external/mex-main/src/cli.ts` next. Trace the user-facing layers in order: `mex init`, `mex check`, `mex sync`, `mex pattern add`, `mex watch`, and `mex commands`. Use this to anchor later claims about the relationship between setup, drift detection, targeted sync, and scaffold growth.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260448:10. Examine the next three drift checkers individually: `src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts`, `src/drift/checkers/command.ts`, and `src/drift/checkers/dependency.ts`. Capture the exact thresholds and matching rules, including how staleness uses both days and commit counts, how commands are validated, and how dependency claims are compared to manifests.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260457:    Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/mex-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around knowledge scaffold design, drift detection, staleness checking, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project memory.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260459:15. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260468:3. .opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260501:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260955:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260961:Mex is a structured markdown scaffold with a TypeScript/Node.js CLI for persistent AI project memory. Instead of a database, it keeps project knowledge in navigable markdown files such as `AGENTS.md`, `ROUTER.md`, `context/*.md`, and `patterns/*.md`. Its defining mechanism is drift detection: eight static checkers validate scaffold claims against the real codebase without AI or token spend. Those checkers cover path validation, frontmatter edges, pattern index consistency, staleness based on git history, command existence, dependency claims, cross-file contradictions, and script coverage. Drift starts at score `100` and deducts `-10` per error, `-3` per warning, and `-1` per info. The scaffold is designed to grow after real work through pattern creation, targeted context updates, and current-state refreshes rather than one-time setup alone.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260974:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260982:5. Read `external/mex-main/src/cli.ts` next. Trace the user-facing layers in order: `mex init`, `mex check`, `mex sync`, `mex pattern add`, `mex watch`, and `mex commands`. Use this to anchor later claims about the relationship between setup, drift detection, targeted sync, and scaffold growth.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260987:10. Examine the next three drift checkers individually: `src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts`, `src/drift/checkers/command.ts`, and `src/drift/checkers/dependency.ts`. Capture the exact thresholds and matching rules, including how staleness uses both days and commit counts, how commands are validated, and how dependency claims are compared to manifests.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260996:    Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/mex-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around knowledge scaffold design, drift detection, staleness checking, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project memory.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:260998:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261007:3. Are Mex's staleness thresholds (`30` warning days, `90` error days, `50` warning commits, `200` error commits) reasonable defaults, and how might similar thresholds be calibrated for `Code_Environment/Public`?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261012:8. How does Mex's targeted sync flow convert static drift findings into precise AI repair prompts, and could `Code_Environment/Public` adopt a similar "fix only what's broken" pattern?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261013:9. How do Mex's GROW rules and `pattern add` workflow support scaffold auto-growth after tasks rather than only during initialization?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261020:- Do analyze how scaffold growth happens after tasks through GROW rules, `pattern add`, targeted sync, and current-state updates.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261040:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261987:- Questions answered: the only clear `adopt now` item is static reference integrity built on typed claim extraction. Freshness is the next best candidate, but only with explicit degraded/unknown semantics. Repair briefing is worth building as a new Spec Kit feature. Command/dependency/script parity needs a separate inventory abstraction first. Auto-growth and a single drift score should stay out.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261995:2. Sprint 2: prototype freshness as a supplemental signal with `exact` vs `unknown/degraded` provenance, using both days and commit-count thresholds and explicit git-failure surfacing.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:261998:5. Keep rejected: automatic pattern/context growth from task completion, and any single-number health score as the primary truth surface.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262038:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262044:Mex is a structured markdown scaffold with a TypeScript/Node.js CLI for persistent AI project memory. Instead of a database, it keeps project knowledge in navigable markdown files such as `AGENTS.md`, `ROUTER.md`, `context/*.md`, and `patterns/*.md`. Its defining mechanism is drift detection: eight static checkers validate scaffold claims against the real codebase without AI or token spend. Those checkers cover path validation, frontmatter edges, pattern index consistency, staleness based on git history, command existence, dependency claims, cross-file contradictions, and script coverage. Drift starts at score `100` and deducts `-10` per error, `-3` per warning, and `-1` per info. The scaffold is designed to grow after real work through pattern creation, targeted context updates, and current-state refreshes rather than one-time setup alone.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262057:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262065:5. Read `external/mex-main/src/cli.ts` next. Trace the user-facing layers in order: `mex init`, `mex check`, `mex sync`, `mex pattern add`, `mex watch`, and `mex commands`. Use this to anchor later claims about the relationship between setup, drift detection, targeted sync, and scaffold growth.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262070:10. Examine the next three drift checkers individually: `src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts`, `src/drift/checkers/command.ts`, and `src/drift/checkers/dependency.ts`. Capture the exact thresholds and matching rules, including how staleness uses both days and commit counts, how commands are validated, and how dependency claims are compared to manifests.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262079:    Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/mex-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around knowledge scaffold design, drift detection, staleness checking, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project memory.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262081:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262090:3. Are Mex's staleness thresholds (`30` warning days, `90` error days, `50` warning commits, `200` error commits) reasonable defaults, and how might similar thresholds be calibrated for `Code_Environment/Public`?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262095:8. How does Mex's targeted sync flow convert static drift findings into precise AI repair prompts, and could `Code_Environment/Public` adopt a similar "fix only what's broken" pattern?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262096:9. How do Mex's GROW rules and `pattern add` workflow support scaffold auto-growth after tasks rather than only during initialization?
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262103:- Do analyze how scaffold growth happens after tasks through GROW rules, `pattern add`, targeted sync, and current-state updates.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262123:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262146:- staleness thresholds and git-based freshness signals
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262147:- targeted sync prompt generation
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262161:Rank findings in this order: drift-detection value for `Code_Environment/Public`, documentation-to-code synchronization impact, scaffold simplicity-to-maintainability ratio, staleness-signal usefulness, compatibility with existing Spec Kit workflows, implementation cost, and cross-phase differentiation from phases `001`, `003`, and `004`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262168:- findings that classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262178:- recommendation: `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262201:- Questions answered: the only clear `adopt now` item is static reference integrity built on typed claim extraction. Freshness is the next best candidate, but only with explicit degraded/unknown semantics. Repair briefing is worth building as a new Spec Kit feature. Command/dependency/script parity needs a separate inventory abstraction first. Auto-growth and a single drift score should stay out.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262209:2. Sprint 2: prototype freshness as a supplemental signal with `exact` vs `unknown/degraded` provenance, using both days and commit-count thresholds and explicit git-failure surfacing.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262212:5. Keep rejected: automatic pattern/context growth from task completion, and any single-number health score as the primary truth surface.

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262240:- **Recommendation**: adopt now

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262286:- **What it does**: Mex’s standout mechanism is typed markdown claim extraction plus zero-token path, edge, index, and freshness checking. None of the other four phases contributed a comparably strong docs-to-code integrity layer.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262288:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262293:- **What it does**: `mex sync` turns failed drift checks into bounded repair briefs with local file context and recent diffs, while `pattern add`, scaffold GROW defaults, and the single drift score push partially trusted content and compressed health reporting too early.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262294:- **Why it matters**: The repair loop fits Public’s packet-local remediation style very well. The auto-growth and score-first pieces cut directly against Public’s trust-sensitive memory model.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262295:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE

.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262458:### Finding 2: Add a supplemental freshness contract with explicit `exact`, `degraded`, and `unknown` provenance
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262459:- **Source**: [staleness.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts), [git.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/git.ts), [session-resume.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-resume.ts), [git-context-extractor.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/extractors/git-context-extractor.ts), [post-save-review.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/core/post-save-review.ts)
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262460:- **What it does**: Mex uses dual thresholds on days and commits, but silently collapses git failures to `null`. Spec Kit already has a better trust precedent in `session_resume`: freshness is surfaced as an explicit contract, not hidden behind a score. The right transplant is a supplemental signal, not a ranker.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262461:- **Why it matters**: We already persist `headRef`, `commitRef`, `repositoryState`, and reviewer guardrails for missing provenance. That gives us the substrate to expose freshness honestly without pretending every result is equally current.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262462:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262464:- **Blueprint files**: create `scripts/lib/memory-freshness.ts`; edit [git-context-extractor.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/extractors/git-context-extractor.ts), [workflow.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/core/workflow.ts), [post-save-review.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/core/post-save-review.ts), [session-types.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/types/session-types.ts), [memory-search.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts), [memory-context.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-context.ts), and [memory-crud-health.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-health.ts).
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262465:- **Function signatures**: `computeFreshnessSignal(args: { filePath: string; projectRoot: string; headRef?: string | null; commitRef?: string | null; repositoryState?: string | null }): Promise<FreshnessSignal>`; `type FreshnessSignal = { provenance: 'exact' | 'degraded' | 'unknown'; ageState: 'fresh' | 'warning' | 'stale' | 'unavailable'; daysSinceChange: number | null; commitsSinceChange: number | null; reason?: string }`; `attachFreshnessTrace(result: Record<string, unknown>, freshness: FreshnessSignal): Record<string, unknown>`.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262466:- **Migration steps**: 1. Compute freshness only when saved git provenance exists; otherwise emit `degraded` or `unknown` with a concrete reason. 2. Reuse Mex’s initial thresholds (`30/90` days, `50/200` commits) but expose them as trace metadata, not ranking weights. 3. Add a reviewer rule that flags missing freshness metadata only when provenance was expected. 4. Surface the signal first in `memory_health` and trace/debug paths, then decide later whether default `memory_context` and `memory_search` responses should show it.
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262472:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:262484:Implement Finding 1 first as a narrow `memory_health` plus `validate.sh` slice, then add Finding 2 as trace-only freshness metadata with explicit degraded/unknown semantics, and only then layer Finding 3 on top of the shared issue schema so repair briefs are generated from real integrity findings rather than a parallel analyzer.
